summary: Teaches that prepositions cannot go at the end of sentences in Spanish, introduces relative clauses with con que/con cual/con quien, demonstrates cuál (which) in questions, and quién/quiénes (who) with plural agreement.

teachings.summary: Unlike English, Spanish never puts prepositions at the end of a sentence. 'Where are you from?' becomes 'From where are you?' (¿De dónde eres?). 'Who I work with' becomes 'with whom I work' (con que trabajo).
teachings.rule: Prepositions must come before their object in Spanish, never at the end of the sentence.

examples.english: Where are you from? (From where are you?)
Where are you going? (To where are you going?)

examples.spanish: No sé con cual quiere hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know which one he wants to do it with

teachings.notes: This doesn't sound formal in Spanish as it might in English - it's simply the only correct option.

teachings.summary: For relative clauses (the girl I work with), Spanish uses: con que, con cual, con la cual, con la que, con quien - all are correct. Que works for everything and is the simplest option.
teachings.rule: Use preposition + que for relative clauses. Alternatives: cual, la cual, la que, quien. Que is sufficient for all cases.

examples.english: The girl I work with
The girl I work with (with whom)

examples.spanish: La chica con la cual trabajo
examples.english: The girl I work with (with which)

examples.spanish: Es la chica con que trabajaba
examples.english: It's the girl I was working with

examples.spanish: La chica con que iba a la fiesta
examples.english: The girl I was going to the party with

teachings.notes: Just use que for everything at this stage. The other options (cual, quien, la que, la cual) will come naturally with exposure.

teachings.summary: Cuál means 'which' and is used in questions. You don't need the word 'one' - cuál alone means 'which one'.

examples.english: Which one do you want?
I don't know which one he wants
I don't know which one he wants to see

examples.spanish: No sé con cuál quiere hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know which one he wants to do it with

teachings.summary: Quién means who and pairs with he/she/it verb forms. Spanish has a plural form quiénes for when you know it's more than one person, which requires the they verb form.
teachings.rule: Quién + singular verb. Quiénes + plural verb. Who = he/she/it form.

examples.english: Who is going?
Who (plural) is going?
Who is here?
Who's there?

examples.spanish: No sé con quién tengo que hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know who I have to do it with

examples.spanish: No sé para quién tengo que hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know who I have to do it for

examples.spanish: No sé por quién tengo que hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know who I have to do it because of

teachings.summary: Instead of only building up from verb roots, you can work backwards from a word you encounter. Seeing 'unidos' (united), identify -ido was added (so -ir or -er verb), remove it to find unir (to unite).
teachings.rule: See a participle or conjugated form -> identify what was added -> remove it to find the infinitive.

examples.english: United -> to unite

teachings.notes: When guessing between -ir and -er, the one you've heard before will feel more natural. This intuition grows with practice.

vocabulary.english: We are going to the United States
